summary: Gruber challenges Snappli's claim that only 4% of iOS 6 users were still using Apple Maps after five days, arguing the data is being badly misinterpreted. Snappli, a data compression proxy service, measured reduced data consumption and concluded users had abandoned Maps, but Gruber points out a far simpler explanation: Apple's new vector-based maps use dramatically less data than Google's old raster tiles. He cites Charles Arthur's Guardian piece showing iOS 6 Maps working entirely offline thanks to superior caching, and Onavo's data showing Apple Maps uses approximately 80% less cellular data than Google Maps for identical tasks. The tech press uncritically ran with Snappli's conclusion despite the obvious confounding variable.
key_points: Snappli's claim that iOS Maps usage plummeted to 4% is based on measuring data consumption through their proxy service, not actual app usage
Apple's switch from raster to vector map tiles means the new Maps app uses up to 80% less data for identical tasks, completely confounding Snappli's methodology
Vector tiles enable superior offline caching — Charles Arthur tracked an entire journey in Korea with zero data connection
Multiple tech outlets uncritically amplified Snappli's flawed conclusion with sensational headlines
Snappli itself is a questionable data source, as all users' cellular traffic passes through their proxy servers
money_quotes: Good thing he didn't make a general conclusion in the headline: 'Just 4% of iOS 6 Users Still Using Apple Maps After 5 Days'. No general conclusions there, no sir.
I'm surprised Apple allows such a thing through the App Store, if for the privacy implications alone — if you're using this, all your cellular data traffic is visible to Snappli.
Everyone knows what turn-by-turn and voice-driven directions are; I suspect most people have no idea what 'vector' map tiles are or how big a difference they can make.

Superior caching and the data-size advantage of vector map tiles seems to me a more likely explanation for the drop in Maps data consumption than Snappli's conclusion that only 4 percent of iOS 6 users are using the app.

The phone tracked our entire journey, with street-level data including the names of shops, all the way. And all the way back. And then, later, out to the airport. All that, without getting a single extra drop of data.
key_insight: Reduced data consumption in Apple Maps reflects the technical superiority of vector tiles over raster graphics, not user abandonment — and the tech press failed to consider this obvious explanation before amplifying a flawed conclusion.
